title: Geometric phases of reduced states in the transverse-field Ising chain
abstract: Geometric phases have been extensively investigated in a wide range of quantum systems, often revealing deep connections to the underlying topology of many-body states. In this work, we examine two geometric phases defined for mixed quantum states$-$the interferometric geometric phase and the Uhlmann phase$-$extracted from two-site reduced density matrices of the transverse-field Ising model with nearest-neighbor interacting spins. By applying coordinated local unitary rotations to the spins, we compute the geometric phases associated with the two-site states across the critical point. We find that the interferometric phase is a more reliable indicator of the quantum phase transition in this model than the Uhlmann phase.
summary: This paper compares the interferometric and Uhlmann geometric phases as indicators of quantum phase transitions in the transverse-field Ising model. It demonstrates that the interferometric phase is a superior order parameter because it correctly reflects the absence of long-range correlations in the paramagnetic phase. The work highlights how different geometric definitions respond uniquely to many-body critical fluctuations.
main_problem: The study investigates whether two types of geometric phases for mixed quantum states—the interferometric geometric phase and the Uhlmann phase—can serve as effective order parameters to identify quantum phase transitions.
main_result: The interferometric geometric phase deviation is found to be a more reliable indicator of the quantum phase transition in the transverse-field Ising model than the Uhlmann phase, as it vanishes for large spin separation in the paramagnetic phase.
method: The authors use Jordan-Wigner, Fourier, and Bogoliubov transformations to diagonalize the Hamiltonian and calculate two-site reduced density matrices via Toeplitz determinants, then compute the geometric phases using local unitary rotations and the purification method.
model_or_system: The transverse-field Ising chain, an exactly solvable model with a Hamiltonian consisting of nearest-neighbor Ising interactions and a transverse magnetic field.
key_observables: Interferometric geometric phase deviation (delta gamma), Uhlmann phase deviation (delta gamma_U), magnetization, and spin-spin correlation functions.
important_parameters: Transverse field strength (lambda), inter-site distance (r), and rotation angle (theta).
main_assumptions: The study focuses on the zero-temperature ground state in the thermodynamic limit (N to infinity).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the interferometric phase deviation as a function of lambda for various distances; Figure 2 shows the Uhlmann phase deviation dependence on lambda, distance, and angle; Figure 3 compares the sensitivity of both phases near the critical point.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of geometric phases as order parameters, describes the Ising model and the mathematical framework for density matrices, presents the calculation of both geometric phases, compares their behavior across the phase transition, and concludes with a discussion on their inequivalent structures.
why_interesting: This is interesting for many-body dynamics and open quantum systems because it explores how geometric structures in mixed-state density matrices encode information about quantum criticality and long-range correlations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
